Heparin was discontinued, and mixing studies were performed; mixing with normal plasma did not correct the aPTT, and the lupus anticoagulant test was negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Adverse reaction to heparin use was considered because the patient was on prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin — “Differential diagnoses included an adverse reaction due to heparin use…” 2. Antiphospholipid syndrome was considered due to isolated aPTT prolongation, but testing was not supportive — “the lupus anticoagulant test was negative.” 3. An acquired factor VIII inhibitor was suspected because the aPTT did not correct on mixing with normal plasma — “Mixing with normal plasma did not correct the aPTT… The presence of an acquired inhibitor of factor VIII was then suspected.”

title: Pulmonary nodules associated with pulmonary embolism: A rare and misleading presentation of amyloidosis
case_prompt: A 74-year-old woman presented with a 2-day history of dyspnea and bilateral chest pain, continuous and worse on exertion; she denied cough, hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Her past medical history included glaucoma, hip arthrosis, and cystocele repair. She was a non-smoker with no relevant exposures. Medications: boric acid and timolol eye drops. Physical examination was normal. Laboratory studies showed WBC 4860/μl, hemoglobin 133 g/l, platelets 205 000/μl, normal PT and aPTT, D-dimer 5360 ng/ml, calcium 2.35 mEq/l, albumin 4.3 U/l, potassium 4.3 mEq/l, CRP 3.4 mg/l, creatinine 0.852 mg/dl, LDH 306 UI/l, and normal liver tests. Chest radiography revealed multiple bilateral nodules. CT pulmonary angiography confirmed bilateral pulmonary emboli and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, some calcified, the largest measuring 35 mm in the right middle lobe. She was anticoagulated with enoxaparin. Flexible bronchoscopy was normal; bronchoalveolar lavage was nondiagnostic, with no AFB on microscopy and negative cultures for bacteria, nocardia, mycobacteria, and fungus. CT-guided lung biopsy demonstrated amorphous eosinophilic material with surrounding plasma cells and no neoplastic cells. 18F-FDG PET-CT showed intense uptake in all pulmonary nodules (SUVmax 8.8) without hypermetabolic lesions elsewhere. Autoimmune serologies were negative (ANA, ANCA); ACE was normal. Serum and urine protein electrophoresis were normal, but immunofixation revealed an isolated monoclonal kappa IgM. Bone marrow biopsy showed 3% plasma cells and 9% lymphocytes with slightly reduced cellularity. Pulmonary function tests and arterial blood gases were normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Metastatic neoplasms were the first hypothesis because of nodular lesions with high FDG uptake — “In our case, because of imaging findings mimicking metastatic lesions and increased FDG uptake, malignancy was the first hypothesis.” 2. Autoimmune disease was in the differential but was ruled out by negative autoantibodies — “Anti-nuclear antibody and anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody were negative.” 3. Infectious disease was considered but excluded by negative BAL cultures for bacteria, nocardia, mycobacteria, and fungus — “There was no acid fast bacilli on microscopic exam and cultures were negative for common bacteria, nocardia, mycobacteria or fungus.” 4. Monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance (MGUS) was identified on immunofixation — “We only found an isolated monoclonal Kappa Immunoglobulin M on immunofixation.”

title: 54‐cm enteric duplication cyst in a 13‐year‐old female
case_prompt:
A 13-year-old Sudanese girl presented with a 2-day history of colicky abdominal pain, absolute constipation, and vomiting of all ingested food. Since age 5, she had recurrent nocturnal abdominal pain exacerbated by spicy foods, frequent yellowish diarrhea, dyspepsia, and daily loud audible bowel sounds. She is the seventh of 10 siblings, with two of her siblings and four nephews having died in early childhood. 

On examination, she appeared ill, in pain, and tachypneic, with a low-grade fever. Her abdomen was distended and tender. Laboratory investigations were within normal limits. 

Supine abdominal radiography showed a tubular gas-filled structure originating in the right iliac fossa, crossing obliquely upward to the left hypochondrium. Erect abdominal radiography revealed multiple air-fluid levels. A diagnosis of acute intestinal obstruction was made, and the patient was taken for emergency exploratory laparotomy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Mesenteric cyst (enteric, mesothelial, or omental) — “Other probable differential diagnoses of EDC may be mesenteric cysts (eg, enteric, mesothelial, or omental cysts).” 2. Gastrointestinal stromal tumor — “A gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) is a soft tissue mass; it may turn into a cyst form if it is complicated by necrosis or cavitation.” 3. Gastrointestinal teratoma — “Gastrointestinal teratomas are rare tumors that perhaps affect young children.” 4. Enteric duplication cyst — classic criteria include “intimate attachment to the GIT,” “a layer of smooth muscle in its wall,” and “an epithelial lining resembling any part of the GIT,” arising “from the mesenteric border of the bowel” and sharing “GIT blood supply.”

title: A case of chlamydia psittaci caused severe pneumonia and meningitis diagnosed by metagenome next-generation sequencing and clinical analysis: a case report and literature review
case_prompt:
A previously healthy woman was admitted with a 20-day history of recurrent headache and fever and a 3-day history of dyspnea. Twenty days before admission, she developed intermittent left occipital headache lasting 2–3 minutes per episode, followed by low-grade fever, sore throat, and dry cough. Initial outpatient treatment provided only transient relief. Fifteen days before admission, fever recurred (to 38 °C), with marked fatigue, persistent dry cough, nausea, and non-projectile vomiting. One week before admission, she noted memory loss, bilateral hearing loss, and limb weakness; urinary incontinence developed the next day without altered consciousness. Three days before admission, dyspnea worsened, and she was transferred to the MICU after rapid progression to severe hypoxemia and lethargy, requiring intubation and mechanical ventilation.

Her medical history was notable for hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine) and chronic hepatitis B (on entecavir), with normal thyroid and liver function pre-illness. She had no diabetes, head trauma, psychiatric disease, tuberculosis, or malignancy. She denied smoking, drug abuse, recent travel, tick bites, and any known contact with birds, poultry, or horses.

On MICU admission (day 1): temperature 39.0 °C; pulse 128 beats/min; respiratory rate 20 breaths/min; blood pressure 105/78 mmHg on dopamine infusion; SpO₂ 96.4% on FiO₂ 0.90. She appeared lethargic under sedation. Lung auscultation revealed coarse breath sounds and bilateral wet rales; no murmurs, hepatosplenomegaly, rash, or definitive neck stiffness; Kernig sign was equivocal.

Laboratory results: WBC 6.37 ×10⁹/L (neutrophils 94.7%); CRP 175 mg/L; PCT 6.87 ng/mL; ESR 65 mm/h; lactate 1.3 mmol/L; albumin 29.4 g/L; AST 218 U/L; ALT 97 U/L. Arterial blood gas: pH 7.378, PaO₂ 79.4 mmHg, PaCO₂ 32.8 mmHg, PaO₂/FiO₂ ≈ 88.2. Interferon-gamma release assay was negative. Hepatitis B serologies showed HBsAg+, HBeAb+, HBcAb+; HBV DNA 610 IU/mL. Tests for influenza A and B viruses, cytomegalovirus, Epstein-Barr virus, Toxoplasma, rubella virus, and herpes simplex virus were negative. Cryptococcal antigen, galactomannan, and β-D-glucan tests were negative. Autoimmune panels (ANA, anti-ENA, ANCA) were negative. Lymphocyte subsets: CD3 76/mm³, CD4 44/mm³, CD8 28/mm³. 

Lumbar puncture: opening pressure 212 mm H₂O; CSF clear; WBC 6 ×10⁶/L; RBC 124 ×10⁶/L; protein 0.267 g/L; glucose 5.83 mmol/L; chloride 125.9 mmol/L; ADA 1 U/L. Abdominal ultrasound was unremarkable. Chest CT showed multiple patchy shadows in both lungs with bilateral pleural effusions. Head CT was normal. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures during febrile episodes were negative.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Viral infections were considered but excluded — “Tests for influenza A virus, influenza B virus, cytomegalovirus, Epstein Barr virus, Toxoplasma, rubella virus, and herpes simplex virus were all negative.” 2. Fungal infection was considered but excluded — “Cryptococcal antigen was negative. Galacto Mannan test and β-D-Glucan test were both negative.” 3. Autoimmune interstitial pneumonia was considered but excluded — “All the indicators were negative for routine laboratory screening (anti-nuclear antibody, anti-extractable nuclear antigen antibody, and anti-neutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody, et al) for autoimmune diseases.” 4. Typical bacterial pneumonia was considered but excluded — “Results of two sets of peripheral blood cultures during episodes of fever were negative.”

title: Intravesical bacillus Calmette–Guérin-induced myopathy presenting as rhabdomyolysis: a case report
case_prompt:
A 72-year-old woman was referred for progressive symmetrical proximal muscle weakness. Two months earlier, she had undergone transurethral resection for early bladder cancer and then began weekly intravesical BCG instillations. Eight days after the third instillation, she noted abrupt leg weakness that gradually worsened over the next three weeks until she was bedridden.

Her medical history included dyslipidemia treated with rosuvastatin for over two years and treated rectal and endometrial cancers without recurrence. On readmission, vital signs were stable. Neurologic examination showed symmetric proximal weakness (modified MRC grade 2/5 in arms, 3/5 in legs), with normal reflexes and sensation; she denied myalgia or urine‐color changes.

Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 6.7 g/dL, WBC 12,850/μL (89% neutrophils), AST 2,235 IU/L, ALT 1,189 IU/L, BUN 146.4 mg/dL, creatinine 5.74 mg/dL, potassium 8.0 mmol/L, and CK >13,000 IU/L. Nerve conduction studies were normal; EMG showed short‐duration, small‐amplitude motor units without spontaneous activity. Muscle MRI demonstrated diffuse T2 hyperintensity in skeletal muscles and subcutaneous fat infiltration. Biopsy of the vastus lateralis revealed variability in fiber size with splitting, type 1 fiber atrophy, mild lymphocytic infiltration, and faint sarcolemmal staining for MHC-1 and C5b–C9 in non‐necrotic fibers.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Attributing to BCG instillation — “Based on these assumptions, a diagnosis of drug-induced myopathy associated with BCG instillation was made.”

title: Upper septal transformation of verapamil-sensitive idiopathic left ventricular tachycardia during catheter ablation: Wolf in sheep׳s clothing?☆
case_prompt:
A 35‐year‐old man with a 5‐year history of recurrent palpitations and multiple emergency department visits. ECGs during episodes demonstrated a wide‐complex tachycardia (QRS ~130 ms) with right bundle branch block morphology and left‐axis deviation. Intravenous verapamil consistently terminated the tachycardia. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a structurally normal heart.

During electrophysiological study, catheters were placed in the high right atrium, His‐bundle region, right ventricular apex, and coronary sinus. Baseline conduction intervals were normal, with no evidence of dual AV nodal physiology, manifest preexcitation, or concealed accessory pathway conduction. The clinical tachycardia was reproducibly induced by right ventricular burst pacing and isoprenaline infusion. It was regular with a cycle length of 290 ms and a QRS duration of 135 ms showing right bundle branch block and left‐axis deviation. There was 1:1 ventriculoatrial association with earliest atrial activation near the His bundle and a negative HV interval. Atrial pacing at a cycle length much shorter than the tachycardia cycle length dissociated atrial from ventricular activity, whereas atrial overdrive pacing demonstrated progressive fusion consistent with entrainment. Based on these findings and consistent termination with overdrive RV pacing and verapamil, a diagnosis of the left posterior fascicular variant of ILVT was made.

An octapolar catheter was positioned retrogradely to map the left ventricular septum during tachycardia. Presystolic Purkinje potentials were recorded 28 ms before QRS onset in the distal third of the left posterior apical septum. Radiofrequency energy was applied to the mid‐apical posterior interventricular septum. Immediately during energy delivery, the wide‐complex tachycardia (QRS width 135 ms; cycle length 299 ms) abruptly transitioned to a regular narrow‐complex tachycardia (QRS width 85 ms; cycle length 281 ms) with 1:1 ventriculoatrial conduction, a relatively short VA interval, and concentric atrial activation.

What is the most likely mechanism and diagnosis of this narrow‐complex tachycardia?
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. LPF variant of ILVT — “Based on these electrophysiological findings… consistent termination with overdrive right ventricular pacing and verapamil infusion, a diagnosis of the LPF variant of ILVT was made.” 2. AV nodal reentrant tachycardia — “No evidence of dual atrioventricular (AV) nodal physiology…” 3. Accessory pathway–mediated tachycardia — “No evidence of manifest preexcitation of the ventricular myocardium… or concealed accessory pathway conduction.”

title: Granulomatous hepatitis due to Bartonella henselae infection in an immunocompetent patient
case_prompt:
A 36-year-old woman with no prior liver disease presented in August 2008 with a 5-day history of constant, sharp right upper quadrant abdominal pain associated with nausea and fatigue but no vomiting. Initial labs showed an ALT of 42 IU/L. Right upper quadrant ultrasound demonstrated hepatomegaly and steatosis. Two weeks later she returned with worsening abdominal pain, low-grade fevers, nausea, and vomiting. MRI of the abdomen revealed a 2 cm enhancing lesion in the right hepatic lobe. Fine-needle biopsy showed a nonspecific mixed inflammatory infiltrate and steatohepatitis without evidence of malignancy; fungal and mycobacterial cultures were negative, and she was discharged without treatment.

Two months later she presented again with worsening right upper quadrant pain and fever. Abdominal CT revealed a 4.8 × 4.7 cm mass in the right hepatic lobe suspicious for malignancy, prompting a partial right hepatectomy. Histopathology revealed florid necrotizing granulomatous inflammation with pseudotumor formation; immunohistochemical stains for mycobacteria, fungal organisms, and cytomegalovirus were negative. Her symptoms initially resolved.

In April 2009, she experienced recurrent right upper quadrant pain with low-grade fevers. An extensive evaluation—including serologies for acute viral hepatitis, toxoplasmosis, HIV, Entamoeba histolytica, Borrelia burgdorferi, Bartonella spp.; testing for urinary Histoplasma and Blastomyces antigens and cryptococcal antigens; and tuberculin skin testing with Quantiferon—was entirely negative. A random liver biopsy showed mild steatosis and nonspecific chronic inflammation; special stains and cultures remained negative.

In July 2009, she again developed right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and fevers up to 102.4 °F. CT of the abdomen revealed multiple low-attenuation lesions in both hepatic lobes. On transfer, she was afebrile with RUQ tenderness; labs showed AST 93 IU/L, ALT 74 IU/L, alkaline phosphatase 193 IU/L, and total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL. Blood, urine, and stool cultures were negative. ANA was 1:40 and anti-mitochondrial antibody was negative. On hospital day 3 she developed a fever of 101.1 °F. A percutaneous liver biopsy revealed extensive granulomatous hepatitis with occasional fibrin rings on a background of mixed micro- and macrovesicular steatosis.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Fungal and mycobacterial infections were considered but “immuno-histochemical stains for mycobacteria, fungal organisms, and cytomegalovirus were negative.” 2. Viral hepatitis was ruled out as “serologies for acute viral hepatitis were negative.” 3. Toxoplasmosis was excluded since “qualitative antibodies for toxoplasmosis … were negative.” 4. HIV infection was excluded because “qualitative antibodies for … human immunodeficiency virus … were negative.” 5. Entamoeba histolytica infection was excluded as “Entamoeba histolytica … were negative.” 6. Lyme disease was unlikely because “an EIA for Borrelia burgdorferi antibodies was negative.” 7. Endemic fungal infections were excluded by “testing for urinary histoplasmosis and blastomycosis antigens and cryptococcus serum antigens were negative.” 8. Tuberculosis was ruled out as “skin testing for tuberculosis and quantiferon gold were negative.” 9. Bartonella infection remained a consideration because “based on the striking features of her hepatic granulomas, the possibility of hepatic Bartonella infection was raised, despite the negative Bartonella antibody titers.”

title: Eosinophilic gastritis without peripheral eosinophilia and atopy: A case report
case_prompt:
A 65‐year‐old woman presented with two months of persistent left upper‐quadrant abdominal pain and multiple episodes of nonbilious, nonbloody vomiting of recently ingested food. The pain worsened with eating, leading to a 5 kg weight loss. She denied fever, night sweats, skin lesions, known allergies, atopy, or family history of similar illness. Initial evaluation elsewhere included an upper gastrointestinal endoscopy that revealed an antral ulcer; no biopsy was taken. Empiric clarithromycin‐based and then levofloxacin‐based Helicobacter pylori regimens were prescribed without improvement. 

On examination, she was hemodynamically stable, with conjunctival pallor and tenderness in the left upper quadrant; no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or ascites were noted. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL, total leukocyte count and differential within normal limits, and an absolute eosinophil count of 147/mm3. A fecal occult blood test was positive. 

A repeat upper endoscopy demonstrated a large ulcer on the lesser curvature of the antrum. Contrast‐enhanced CT of the abdomen showed focal, asymmetrical thickening of the gastric wall (6.5 mm thickness over a 2.6 cm length) along the lesser curvature without perigastric lymphadenopathy.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Chronic gastritis with malignancy was first considered given the patient’s age, chronicity of symptoms, nonspecific presentation, and failure to respond to H. pylori therapy — “Our working diagnosis was chronic gastritis with a suspicion of malignancy based on age, chronicity, nonspecific symptomatology, and no‐response to prior treatment with clarithromycin and consecutive therapy with levofloxacin‐based H. pylori regimen.”

title: An Unusual Case of Suspected Microvascular Angina in a Newborn
case_prompt:
A term female newborn (3.700 kg) was born vaginally without prenatal complications. Family history was negative for heart disease. Before discharge, transthoracic echocardiography showed moderate tricuspid regurgitation due to valvular dysplasia; all other findings were normal. She was discharged for follow-up.

On day 6 of life she was admitted with tachypnea, sweating, and failure to thrive; weight was 3.220 kg. Signs of heart failure and respiratory distress were present: O2 saturation 96% on 6 L/min, venous blood gas revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.03, pO2 46 mmHg, pCO2 29 mmHg, HCO3– 12.3 mmol/L, BE –19). Mechanical ventilation was initiated. On arrival: BP 70/30 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, diuresis preserved. Examination showed a grade 3/6 systolic murmur, weak femoral pulses, and hepatomegaly (liver palpable 3 cm below costal margin).

Laboratory studies: high-sensitivity troponin T 1001 ng/L, CK-MB 37.4 mcg/L; venous blood gas on admission pH 7.1, pO2 65 mmHg, pCO2 28 mmHg, lactate 14.8 mmol/L. Viral studies were negative. Chest radiograph showed cardiomegaly and mild pulmonary venous congestion.

ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm at 133 bpm, right atrial enlargement, inferior Q waves, and lateral repolarization abnormalities. TTE revealed a normal-sized left ventricle with hypokinesis of the inferior wall, dyskinesis of the septum, and mildly depressed ejection fraction; the right ventricle was enlarged with mildly impaired systolic function. Moderate mitral regurgitation and severe tricuspid regurgitation were present. The right coronary artery origin was normal; the left coronary artery was not well visualized.

Selective coronary angiography confirmed normal origin and anatomy of both coronary arteries and normal hemodynamic parameters. With mechanical ventilation and diuretics, hemodynamics improved and heart failure resolved. Within hours, the patient was weaned from ventilation; ECG normalized except for a prolonged corrected QT interval (480 msec). Cardiac markers fell (high-sensitivity troponin T 163.2 ng/L, CK-MB 5 mcg/L). TTE showed normal biventricular systolic function, no wall motion abnormalities, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and no mitral regurgitation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Congenital coronary anomalies considered — “Angiography showed normal coronary artery origin, thus excluding the most frequent causes of ischemia in this particular population.”

title: A parasitic leiomyoma of the sigmoid mesentery with schwannoma-like image findings
case_prompt: A 29-year-old woman presented with lower abdominal pain. She had undergone laparoscopic myomectomy at age 25, during which uterine leiomyoma fragments were removed with surgical scissors in a collection bag. Four laparoscopic scars were noted; the mass was not palpable. Laboratory studies, including serum CA 19-9 (≤1.2 U/mL), CEA (0.6 ng/mL), CA-125 (9.5 U/mL), and sIL-2R (206 U/mL), were within normal limits, as were other routine tests. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a well-defined 38 × 32 × 42 mm pelvic mass with central enhancement in the early phase and peripheral enhancement in the late phase, with a feeding vessel from the second sigmoidal artery and no evidence of invasion or metastasis. T2-weighted MRI demonstrated high signal intensity at the tumor margins and low signal centrally, while T1-weighted images showed heterogeneous low signal. Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a hypoechoic, heterogeneous mass with a clear capsule and pulsatile internal blood flow. FDG PET–CT showed nodular uptake in the pelvis (SUVmax 3.2) without distant lesions. Colonoscopy was unremarkable. The preoperative differential diagnosis included schwannoma, gastrointestinal stromal tumor, leiomyoma or leiomyosarcoma, desmoid tumor, and solitary fibrous tumor. Because it was difficult to differentiate benign from malignant, surgical resection en bloc was planned without breaching the capsule.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. GIST was included among the mesenchymal differential diagnoses — “the differential diagnoses of the sigmoid mesenteric tumor in the present case included … gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) …”. 2. Leiomyoma was considered because of her history of uterine myoma removal — “leiomyoma was a possible differential diagnosis based on the patient’s history”. 3. Solitary fibrous tumor was included among possible mesenchymal tumors — “the differential diagnoses … included … solitary fibrous tumor.”

title: Acute Limb Ischemia in the Young: A Rare Case of Essential Thrombocytosis
case_prompt: A 37-year-old woman presented with a 1-week history of pain in the toes of her left foot. The pain was persistent, worse with movement, and associated with numbness, tingling, and a bluish-pale discoloration of the left toes. She had no prior medical history or medication use. On examination, heart rate was 102 beats/min; left femoral pulsation was weakened, distal pulses were absent, and the left toes were dry, cold, and pale. Motor function was intact, but sensation was reduced in the left foot. Electrocardiogram, chest radiograph, and transthoracic echocardiography showed no abnormalities or intracardiac thrombus. Doppler ultrasound demonstrated triphasic flow in the right superficial femoral artery, and a thrombus in the left common femoral artery with poor distal flow. Laboratory studies revealed a platelet count of 812 × 10^3/μL; other routine blood tests were normal. Peripheral blood smear showed marked thrombocytosis with giant platelets, leukocytosis, and immature granulocytes. A JAK2 mutation assay was obtained.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Progressive arterial disease was unlikely — “Progressive arterial disease deemed unlikely due to her young age and no intracardiac thrombus on echocardiography.” 2. Cardiac embolization was excluded — “transthoracic echocardiography revealed no abnormality or intracardiac thrombus.” 3. Primary myelofibrosis was unlikely — “In PMF ... anemia is typically present and increases over time accompanied by mild to moderate leukocytosis, mild to marked thrombocytosis, abnormal cytokine expression, cachexia, and hepatosplenomegaly.”

title: Hepatic Angiomyolipoma: A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_prompt: A 50-year-old non-smoker Caucasian woman with no past medical history presented with chronic mild right upper quadrant abdominal pain for one year. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies, including complete blood count, liver function tests, and alpha-fetoprotein level, were within normal limits. Viral hepatitis B and C serologies were negative. An abdominal ultrasound showed a 15 mm homogeneous hyperechoic nodule in segment VI of the liver. Contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a well-circumscribed 17 mm nodule in segment VI that was moderately hyperintense on T2-weighted images, hypointense on T1-weighted images, and hyperintense on diffusion-weighted images with apparent diffusion coefficient restriction. The lesion demonstrated arterial-phase enhancement, washout in the portal and delayed phases, capsule enhancement, and a signal drop on opposed-phase imaging, indicative of fat content.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Fat-containing hepatocellular carcinoma was considered because of the hypervascular steatotic appearance with arterial enhancement and washout — “radiological presentation was consistent with a hypervascular steatotic nodule; therefore, fat-containing hepatocellular carcinoma and hepatocellular adenoma were the main considered diagnoses.” 2. Hepatocellular adenoma was considered due to the similar imaging pattern of a hypervascular fat-containing hepatic lesion — “radiological presentation was consistent with a hypervascular steatotic nodule; therefore, fat-containing hepatocellular carcinoma and hepatocellular adenoma were the main considered diagnoses.” 3. Hepatocellular carcinoma was deemed less likely in the absence of underlying liver disease — “HCC was deemed less likely given that the patient had no signs of portal hypertension or hepatocellular dysfunction.” 4. Metastases from gastrointestinal stromal tumors were included in the differential for hypervascular hepatic lesions — “several differential diagnoses are to be considered such as HCC, hepatocellular adenoma, metastases from gastrointestinal stromal tumors, and focal steatosis.” 5. Focal steatosis was considered because fat-containing areas can mimic nodular hepatic lesions on imaging — “several differential diagnoses are to be considered such as HCC, hepatocellular adenoma, metastases from gastrointestinal stromal tumors, and focal steatosis.”

title: Congenital anterolateral tibial bowing and polydactyly: a case report
case_prompt: A 15-month-old boy was referred for evaluation of a right lower limb deformity. He was born at term following induction; growth parameters were normal. On examination, the right foot had medial splaying and duplication of the hallux with mild anterolateral bowing of the right tibia. The left index finger had radial deviation. There were no café-au-lait macules, neurofibromas, or other cutaneous stigmata of a neurocutaneous disorder. Initial radiographs of the right leg showed anterolateral bowing of the tibia, a medullary cyst, and duplication of the first ray; the fibula appeared straight. At 7 months of age, an MRI of the brain and right lower extremity was performed: the brain was normal; the right tibia showed midshaft cortical thinning with continuous anterior cortex and posterior callus formation without evidence of pseudarthrosis or medullary canal duplication; a supernumerary digit arising from the first cuneiform was noted.
diagnostic_reasoning: 2. Congenital pseudarthrosis was considered in the differential diagnosis, but excluded as MRI demonstrated “no definitive evidence of duplication of the tibial medullary canal nor was a pseudarthrosis identified.” 3. Camptomelic dysplasia and metabolic bone diseases were considered among causes of congenital bowing, but excluded as they typically show “symmetric involvement” while this case was unilateral. 4. CATBP was supported by the combination of anterolateral tibial bowing, hallux duplication, and hand anomalies, as the patient “presented with congenital anterolateral tibial bowing associated with hand and foot anomalies.”
final_diagnosis: Congenital anterolateral tibial bowing and polydactyly

title: Disseminated Tuberculosis Presenting as Chronic Orchiepididymitis in a Military Trainee: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_prompt:
A 23-year-old man from Cameroon presented with acute-onset, right-sided scrotal pain and swelling while in the United States for military training. He denied any current or prior sexual activity, penile discharge, hematuria, fevers, night sweats, weight loss, or cough.  
Initial urinalysis showed pyuria; no urine culture was performed. Urine nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) was negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis. He was diagnosed with acute epididymitis and treated with ceftriaxone and doxycycline, with symptomatic improvement. Six weeks later, he had recurrent pain; scrotal ultrasound again showed epididymitis. Repeat urinalysis (no culture) and gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT were negative, and he received a 30-day course of ciprofloxacin, which improved the pain but not the swelling.  
Five months after the initial presentation, he returned with another recurrence. On examination, there was prominent right scrotal swelling and tenderness. Ultrasound demonstrated right epididymitis with possible necrosis, a complex hydrocele, and a focal scrotal abscess. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed a complex right scrotal fluid collection, an enlarged heterogeneous prostate, an enlarged juxtaesophageal lymph node, and right apical lung nodular scarring. Although TB was considered in the differential diagnosis, a unilateral orchiectomy was performed because malignancy was also high on the differential given his age.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis infection — “urine nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) was negative for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis.” 2. Testicular malignancy — “malignancy was also high on the differential given his age.” 3. Genitourinary tuberculosis — “Although TB was considered in the differential diagnosis, a unilateral orchiectomy was performed...”

title: Successful Thrombolytic Therapy for Mechanical Mitral Valve Thrombosis Using Bivalirudin-Based Thrombolytic Regimen
case_prompt:
A 43-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnea. She was afebrile, with a heart rate of 87 beats per minute, blood pressure of 97/70 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 94% on room air. Physical examination revealed elevated jugular venous pressure and a muffled mechanical closure click. Her medical history was notable for rheumatic heart disease, mitral valve replacement with a 27-mm bileaflet mechanical prosthesis 9 years earlier, a prior episode of prosthetic valve thrombosis treated with tPA 8 years ago, and a reported allergy to porcine products. Home warfarin therapy was chronic, but her INR on admission was subtherapeutic at 1.3.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL and brain natriuretic peptide 241 pg/mL. Electrocardiography revealed normal sinus rhythm with nonspecific ST-segment changes. Chest radiography showed mild interstitial prominence. CT scan of the chest with contrast demonstrated bilateral moderate pleural effusions and an enlarged pulmonary trunk. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 50% and significant stenosis across the mechanical mitral valve with restricted leaflet motion; the mean mitral gradient was 23 mm Hg at a heart rate of 79 beats per minute. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed an immobile anterior leaflet and restricted posterior leaflet with a mean gradient of 22 mm Hg at 78 beats per minute, and it showed an echo-dense mass on the anterior leaflet. Further CT angiography identified a possible thrombus measuring ≤1.0 cm in greatest dimension attached to the mitral valve.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Prosthetic valve thrombosis considered — “The recent acute change in our patient’s symptoms and the subtherapeutic INR were the clues in favor of a possible thrombus…” 2. Acute nonvalvular heart failure exacerbation considered and excluded — “Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed … significant stenosis across the mechanical mitral valve with restricted leaflet motion.” 3. Pulmonary emboli considered and excluded — “Computed tomography scan of the chest with contrast material demonstrated … enlarged pulmonary trunk,” and CT angiogram instead revealed a mass attached to the valve. 4. Pannus formation considered and excluded — “Although pannus had not been ruled out, the combination of the subtherapeutic INR and the echocardiogram and CT findings led the team in this case to suspect thrombus as the cause of the obstruction.”

title: Transient Oculomotor Palsy after Influenza Vaccination: Short Report
case_prompt:
A 79-year-old white man presented with sudden onset of right-sided ophthalmoplegia, diplopia, and ptosis accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and headache. The symptoms began two days after receiving an inactivated seasonal influenza vaccine containing A/New Caledonia/20/99 (H1N1)-like, A/Wisconsin/67/2005 (H3N2)-like, and B/Malaysia/2506/2004-like viruses. 

He had no history of diabetes, glucose intolerance, arterial hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, systemic vasculitis, smoking, obesity, or other vascular risk factors. There was no personal or family history of neurologic disorders. 

On examination, temperature and other vital signs were normal; blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg. Neurologic examination revealed right oculomotor nerve palsy with pupil sparing; all other cranial nerves were intact. Motor strength, reflexes, tone, coordination, sensation, gait, and Romberg test were normal.

Laboratory studies showed normal red and white blood cell counts, platelet count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, electrolytes, renal function, lipid panel, fasting glucose, glucose tolerance, and glycosylated hemoglobin. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Noncontrast head CT, brain MRI, and MR angiography were unremarkable.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Compressive causes such as posterior communicating artery aneurysm were excluded by normal imaging — “Since all possible underlying structural causes of oculomotor nerve injury were ruled out by clinical, laboratorial, and imaging workup.”

title: Nonbacterial Thrombotic Endocarditis of Bioprosthetic Aortic Valve Presenting as Cardioembolic Stroke in a Patient without Predisposing Systemic Disease
case_prompt:
A 73-year-old man with ischemic heart disease and a bioprosthetic aortic valve implanted in 2009, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, stage 4 chronic kidney disease, and peptic ulcer disease presented with a 2-week history of worsening exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and intermittent presyncope. His medications included lifelong clopidogrel, antihypertensives, statin therapy, and insulin.

Initial ECG showed a stable borderline left bundle branch block without ischemic changes. He was admitted for intravenous diuresis. A few days later, he awoke with new right-sided visual disturbance. Vital signs were within normal limits. Neurologically, he had a right homonymous hemianopia; tone, reflexes, cranial nerves, strength, and sensation were otherwise normal. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rate with no rub, gallop, or murmur; there were coarse crepitations at the lung bases and bilateral ankle pitting oedema. Abdominal and dermatologic findings were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 102 g/L, white-cell count 15.3×10^9/L, and C-reactive protein 121 mg/L. Five sets of blood cultures were negative. Serologic tests for Coxiella and Bartonella were unremarkable. Coagulation studies, a prothrombotic panel (protein C, protein S, factor V Leiden), and blood film were normal.

Diffusion-weighted brain MRI demonstrated a left occipital lobe infarct at the junction of the posterior and middle cerebral artery territories without hemorrhage or old infarcts. Transoesophageal echocardiography revealed a 10×7.5 mm heterogeneous mobile echogenicity attached to the bioprosthetic aortic valve with moderate stenosis, severe paravalvular regurgitation, and leaflet degeneration; no valvular abscess was seen. Cardiac positron-emission tomography showed no FDG uptake at the bioprosthetic valve.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Consideration of nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis — “A provisional diagnosis of NBTE was based on presentation of acute ischemic stroke with a non-FDG-avid heterogenous bioprosthetic aortic valve vegetation and negative blood cultures.” 2. Consideration of embolization of a calcific valve fragment — “Embolization of a calcific fragment of the degenerated bioprosthetic valve remained a potential differential diagnosis.”

title: Struma ovarii presenting with Hashimoto's thyroiditis: a case report
case_prompt: A 17-year-old Caucasian woman presented with acute right lower abdominal pain. Ultrasonography and CT revealed a 20-cm hyperdense cystic right adnexal mass with solid components up to the umbilicus. Emergency laparotomy showed torsion of the right ovary; a right salpingo-oophorectomy removed a 1600-g tumor. Histopathology disclosed a benign cystic struma ovarii confirmed by thyroglobulin immunostaining, with lymphocytes and lymphoid follicles among thyroid follicles. Three days after surgery, thyroid function tests showed TSH 0.001 mIU/mL (normal 0.27–4.2), free T4 1.32 ng/dL (normal 0.74–1.52), and free T3 3.6 pg/mL (normal 2.3–4.2). Two months later, she remained asymptomatic but had persistent subclinical hyperthyroidism. Thyroid ultrasound demonstrated a normal-sized gland with mildly heterogeneous echogenicity. Thyroid scintigraphy showed focal uptake in the left lobe with suppression elsewhere, and I-131 uptake was reduced to 16% at 4 hours and 27% at 24 hours.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Malignant transformation in struma ovarii — “Postoperative pathologic examination had revealed a benign cystic struma ovarii, confirmed by immunohistochemical staining for thyroglobulin.” 2. Exogenous thyroid hormone administration — “Struma ovarii are rare among other more likely causes of these findings, such as exogenous thyroid hormone administration and thyroiditis.” 3. Graves’ disease — “Thyroid scintigraphy showed condensed focal accumulation in the left thyroid lobe, while other sides of the left lobe remained mildly suppressed and the right lobe was homogenous.”

title: Iatrogenic aortic valve fenestration rupture following coronary angiography: a case report
case_prompt:
A 66-year-old man with sleep apnoea and a history of junctional tachycardia ablation presented with acute dyspnoea shortly after undergoing urgent percutaneous coronary intervention for an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. His medications included aspirin, clopidogrel, bisoprolol, atorvastatin, and ramipril. Initial examination prior to PCI was unremarkable, and transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) showed no valvular abnormalities. Coronary angiography revealed a complete occlusion of the distal right coronary artery, which was stented without difficulty, despite a shepherd’s crook configuration of the proximal vessel.

Soon after the procedure, he developed shortness of breath and a short decrescendo early diastolic aortic murmur. NT-pro–brain natriuretic peptide was 1784 pg/mL. Chest radiograph was normal, and CT angiography excluded dissection and pulmonary embolism. Repeat TTE showed new moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation (vena contracta 0.6 cm; jet >50% of outflow tract; pressure half-time 340 ms) with an abnormal mobile structure on the aortic valve. Left ventricular ejection fraction was 45% with inferior wall hypokinesis.

Transoesophageal echocardiography identified a 17 mm fine, filamentous mobile lesion attached to the non-coronary cusp, causing a coaptation defect and severe eccentric regurgitant jet. The valve was tricuspid without root dilation. There was no clinical or laboratory evidence of infective endocarditis. After multidisciplinary discussion, the patient was referred for surgical evaluation.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Infective endocarditis considered — “no clinical or laboratory evidence in favour of IE.” 2. Fibroelastoma considered — “Fibroelastoma, on the other hand, is a benign tumour … usually found to be pedunculated and larger than Lambl’s excrescence in echocardiography.” 3. Lambl’s excrescence considered — “Lambl’s excrescence is a degenerative filamentous strand of valvular leaflets that appears as very thin and lint-like mobile threads arising from the free borders or ventricular surfaces of aortic leaflets in echocardiography.”

title: Rituximab and pericardiectomy with waffle procedure in constrictive pericarditis due to IgG4‐related disease: A case report
case_prompt:
An 82-year-old man with chronic atrial fibrillation and type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with a 3-month history of worsening peripheral edema and pleural effusion despite escalating diuretic therapy. He had stable heart failure with preserved ejection fraction for 2 years on bisoprolol, losartan, spironolactone, furosemide, and empagliflozin. Laboratory studies showed total bilirubin increased from 0.9 to 1.7 mg/dL, γ-glutamyl transferase from 32 to 197 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase from 70 to 176 U/L. He developed sialadenitis.  
Electrocardiography revealed chronic atrial fibrillation. Transthoracic echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 50%, biatrial enlargement, a septal bounce, and a 40.1% respiratory variation in left ventricular E-velocity. Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated partial pericardial calcifications without aortic wall thickening or hydronephrosis. Right heart catheterization showed equal right and left ventricular end-diastolic pressures, a dip-and-plateau pattern, and a systolic area index of 2.5.  
Autoimmune serologies—including anti-nuclear, anti-DNA, P-ANCA, C-ANCA, and anti-RNP-1 antibodies—and C-reactive protein were not elevated. QuantiFERON-TB Gold was negative. His serum IgG4 level was 1168 mg/dL, and a minor salivary gland biopsy showed >10 IgG4-positive cells per high-powered field with an IgG4+/IgG-positive cell ratio of about 40%.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Exclusion of retroperitoneal fibrosis or aortitis — “There was no dilatation and thickening of the aortic wall, and hydronephrosis was not detected by contrast-enhanced CT.”

title: Gastrointestinal cryptococcoma – Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome or cryptococcal relapse in a patient with AIDS?
case_prompt: A 37 year-old man with HIV infection and a recent history of cryptococcal meningitis, treated with amphotericin followed by fluconazole, began ART (zidovudine, lamivudine, efavirenz) 21 days after meningitis treatment completion. His baseline CD4 count was 5 cells/µL, rising to 29 cells/µL by day +21 of ART. On day +21, he noted hypogastric abdominal pain and was treated empirically for urinary tract infection with ciprofloxacin, with some improvement. By day +35, the pain recurred, now associated with low-grade fevers and a dry cough; physical examination showed tachypnea (28 breaths/min) and right iliac-fossa tenderness. A complete blood count, chest radiograph, and abdominal ultrasound were unremarkable. He was treated for possible atypical pneumonia with doxycycline; his respiratory symptoms improved, but abdominal pain persisted with occasional vomiting. On day +84, a repeat chest radiograph to exclude tuberculosis was unremarkable. His CD4 count had fallen to 14 cells/µL, and HIV-1 viral load was 22,000 copies/mL despite verified adherence. On day +112, abdominal ultrasound showed an appendicular mass and para-aortic lymphadenopathy; CD4 was 8 cells/µL and viral load 15,475 copies/mL. He consented to ultrasound-guided biopsy on day +140, and a second ultrasound demonstrated ileal wall thickening (6 mm) with adjacent 1 cm lymph nodes. Two lymph-node biopsy specimens showed no abnormality on histology. By day +168, abdominal pain and vomiting persisted, CD4 was 24 cells/µL, viral load 23,755 copies/mL, and he was presumed to have ART failure possibly due to malabsorption from thickened gut. An empiric trial of anti-tuberculosis therapy was started.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Atypical pneumonia suspected for fever and cough; respiratory symptoms improved with doxycycline while abdominal pain continued — “the patient was managed as a possible atypical pneumonia with doxycycline, and his respiratory symptoms improved.” 2. Pulmonary tuberculosis excluded by normal chest radiograph — “another chest radiograph was performed to exclude TB, which was also unremarkable.” 3. Gastrointestinal tuberculosis empirically treated after imaging but lymph-node biopsy was nondiagnostic — “Two biopsy specimens from the lymph nodes demonstrated no abnormality at histology.” 4. Antiretroviral treatment failure hypothesized based on persistent viremia and gut thickening affecting absorption — “The patient was presumed to be failing ART, possibly due to poor absorptive surface because of the thickened gut wall.”

title: Congenital insensitivity to pain and anhydrosis due to a rare mutation and that is complicated by inflammatory bowel disease and amyloidosis: a case report
case_prompt: A 19-year-old male, born in 1990 to consanguineous parents, was referred for evaluation of multisystem involvement. His father had renal failure and familial Mediterranean fever. Beginning at 6 months, he had recurrent fevers and an absence of sweating. At 8 months, with eruption of his first teeth, he began biting his fingers and toes and sustaining tongue and skin injuries. By age 6, he had learning difficulties, multiple bone fractures from repeated trauma, and by age 9 was reported to have Lesch–Nyhan syndrome. At age 10, he underwent multiple hip‐reduction surgeries for recurrent dislocations. By age 16, he had deformed limbs, a sacral decubitus ulcer, and chronic osteomyelitis of the right elbow, both ankles, and both feet; cultures from right‐elbow ulcers grew methicillin‐resistant Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Acinetobacter. At 19 years, he underwent bilateral below-knee amputations. Two months after surgery, he developed ascites. Laboratory evaluation showed uric acid 4.1 mg/dL, creatinine 0.1 mg/dL, serum albumin 1.3 g/dL, ascitic fluid albumin 0.1 g/dL, and a 24-hour urine protein excretion of 6.29 g. Six months later, he developed generalized edema and new-onset diarrhea. Colonoscopy revealed multiple erythematous, ulcerating lesions throughout the colon; biopsy demonstrated severe crypt abscesses, goblet cell depletion, and lamina propria inflammation consistent with idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease suggestive of Crohn’s disease, and mesalamine was initiated. During the same admission, he had generalized tonic–clonic seizures; brain MRI and EEG were normal.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Lesch–Nyhan syndrome was reconsidered when characteristic laboratory and autonomic findings were absent — “we doubted the primary diagnosis of Lesch–Nyhan syndrome because of the normal uric acid level and the history of recurrent fever and decreased sweating which favor the diagnosis of CIPA.”
final_diagnosis: Congenital insensitivity to pain and anhydrosis

title: Atypical Kawasaki disease presenting with macroscopic hematuria in an infant: a case report
case_prompt:
A previously healthy 9-month-old boy was transferred after 8 days of fever and new-onset macroscopic hematuria. He had an initial self-limited diarrheal illness, then developed visible blood and pyuria on repeat urine microscopy. Empiric antibiotics for presumed urinary tract infection produced no defervescence. He had reduced oral intake and irritability but normal development and immunizations. 

On examination, temperature was 103 °F; he was ill-appearing, irritable, and well-hydrated. A 1.5 cm right cervical lymph node was palpable, and a diffuse maculopapular rash was present. No Bacillus Calmette–Guérin reaction was noted. Other systems were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed white blood count 24 × 10^3 cells/mm^3 (80% neutrophils), hemoglobin 8 g/dL, platelets 440 × 10^3 cells/mm^3, C-reactive protein 96 mg/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 80 mm/hr, alanine transaminase 98 IU/dL, aspartate transaminase 120 IU/dL, γ-glutamyl transferase 156 IU/dL, total protein 5.8 g/dL, albumin 2.4 g/dL; renal function, ferritin, and lipid profile were normal. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis was normal. Serologies for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, influenza, and Mycoplasma were negative. Urine, blood, and CSF cultures were sterile. Chest radiograph was normal. Abdominal ultrasound showed mild hepatomegaly. 

On illness day 12, echocardiography demonstrated dilatation of the left coronary artery (4.75 mm).
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Urinary tract infection excluded — “his urine culture was negative.” 2. Viral infection excluded — “Results of serology for Epstein–Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, influenza, and mycoplasma were within normal limits.”

title: Chemical abscess post vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation embolisation with ethylene vinyl alcohol copolymer
case_prompt: An 8-month-old child presented with progressive head enlargement since birth, global developmental delay (developmental age equivalent to 3 months), and cranial bruits. The occipitofrontal circumference was 54 cm (normal 43–45 cm). Brain MRI showed a well-circumscribed, markedly T2-hypointense midline lesion dorsal to the third ventricle with aqueductal obstruction, resulting in dilation of the lateral and third ventricles and a normal fourth ventricle. Digital subtraction angiography confirmed a mural type vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation with feeders from both posterior cerebral arteries and the left anterior cerebral artery. The child underwent transarterial embolisation via Onyx injection into the posteromedial choroidal branches of the right posterior cerebral artery, achieving occlusion of the venous sac. On day 3 post-embolisation MRI showed minimal reduction in sac size, mild peripheral rim enhancement, and no internal restricted diffusion. One-month follow-up MRI demonstrated a residual aneurysmal sac with thick peripheral mural enhancement, marked restricted diffusion, and persistent hydrocephalus.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Onyx granuloma considered — “Brain abscesses, and onyx granuloma formation have been described in case reports as rare complications after endovascular embolisation of brain arteriovenous malformations. They should be considered in the differential diagnosis as their management is different.” 2. Use of diffusion-weighted imaging — “Diffusion-weighted imaging has been recommended to differentiate between abscess and granuloma, with abscess showing restricted diffusion.”

title: Heart failure misdiagnosed as acute cholecystitis: a case report
case_prompt: A 26-year-old man with no history of hypertension or heart disease presented with a 3-day history of upper abdominal pain. He reported epigastric tenderness with rebound and a positive Murphy’s sign. He was afebrile. Laboratory tests showed mild jaundice and a mildly elevated leukocyte count; procalcitonin and C-reactive protein levels were normal. An abdominal CT scan demonstrated a hyperdense gallbladder, mild gallbladder wall swelling, and fluid collections in the chest, pericardial sac, and abdomen. Electrocardiography showed ST-segment depression with T-wave inversion in V1–4 and a prolonged QT interval. The patient was taken to the operating room for an emergency laparotomy; intraoperatively, there was no evidence of bowel perforation, gallbladder enlargement, or cholangitis, but there was ascites and bowel wall edema. Approximately 1000 mL of peritoneal fluid was drained, and the patient was transferred to the intensive care unit.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute cholecystitis was considered because of epigastric pain, rebound tenderness, Murphy’s sign, and a hyperdense gallbladder on CT — “On examination, there was tenderness and rebound tenderness in the epigastric area with Murphy’s sign. The abdomen CT revealed a hyperdense gallbladder.” 2. Acute cholecystitis was excluded intraoperatively when no gallbladder enlargement or cholangitis was seen — “During the operation, the surgeons did not find any evidence of… gallbladder enlargement, or cholangiogram infection.” 3. Bowel perforation was ruled out during surgery — “During the operation, the surgeons did not find any evidence of bowel perforation…” 4. Intra-abdominal infection was deemed unlikely given normal PCT and CRP — “Procalcitonin (pct) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels were normal.”

title: Giant cemento-ossifying fibroma of the mandible
journal: Journal of Natural Science, Biology, and Medicine
case_prompt:
A 34-year-old woman presented with a 6-year history of a painless swelling of the lower jaw, initially 1 cm in diameter on the left, which gradually enlarged to involve both sides of the face. She reported only difficulty in chewing. On examination, there was gross facial asymmetry with fullness of the cheeks and lower lip and obliteration of the mentolabial sulcus and nasolabial folds. A well-demarcated, hard, lobulated, nontender swelling measured approximately 8×6×5 cm over the left cheek and a diffuse hard, lobulated, nontender swelling extended from the left mandibular ramus to the right mandibular body, obliterating the inferior border. No cervical lymphadenopathy was present.

Intraorally, a diffuse, hard, lobulated swelling extended from the left to the right retromolar regions, with bilateral buccal, labial, and lingual cortical expansion and intact mucosa. A provisional diagnosis of a benign fibro-osseous lesion of the mandible was made, and the differential diagnoses included fibrous dysplasia, ossifying/cementifying fibroma, and central giant cell granuloma (non-aggressive).

Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, serum protein, calcium, phosphorus, and alkaline phosphatase levels, were within normal limits.

Panoramic radiograph showed a well-defined mixed radiopaque and radiolucent lesion from the left ramus to the right angle of the mandible, measuring about 11×7 cm, with a “cotton-wool” pattern of calcification, concentric growth producing a ball-like outline, and bowing—but no erosion—of the inferior mandibular border. Occlusal radiograph revealed buccal and lingual cortical plate expansion with lingual displacement of teeth. Posterior–anterior mandible view confirmed a mixed radiopaque–radiolucent expansile lesion extending from the left ramus to the right mandibular angle. Axial and 3D CT scans demonstrated a large (11.8×7.0×7.3 cm) expansile lesion of the right and left mandibular bodies and left ramus, with mixed sclerotic and lucent areas and central low-density foci.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Fibrous dysplasia was considered but is less likely because “the former [fibrous dysplasia] has a poorly defined margin, whereas the latter [cemento-ossifying fibroma] has a well defined margin.” 2. Central giant cell granuloma was questioned given that the lesion shows a “cotton-wool pattern of calcification,” a feature not typical of purely radiolucent central giant cell granulomas.

title: Clinical cases of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis concurrent with hydromyelia
case_prompt:
A 30-year-old man first noted right calf muscle cramps in 2013. Twelve months later, he developed right foot drop, fasciculations in the right leg, and wasting of the right anterior compartment. Neurological examination in 2014 showed 2/5 strength of the right ankle extensors, right leg amyotrophy, brisk reflexes in the lower limbs, a positive right Babinski sign, and no sensory deficits.

By 2015, fasciculations were present throughout the body, painful cramps and weakness appeared in the left shin, and thigh muscle atrophy developed. Deep tendon reflexes remained brisk except for absent Achilles reflexes bilaterally; Hoffmann sign was positive. There were no bulbar or respiratory symptoms.

In 2015, T2-weighted MRI of the thoracic spine demonstrated a slitlike syrinx (central canal dilation) extending from T3 to T9 without Chiari malformation or extramedullary compression; brain and cervical MRI were normal. EMG in 2015 revealed fibrillation potentials, fasciculations, and large‐amplitude, long‐duration motor–unit potentials in both upper and lower limbs without conduction block. GM-1 and GM-2 antibody titers were negative, and routine blood and CSF studies were normal. Follow-up spinal MRI in 2016 and 2019 showed no change in the cavity size.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Spinal muscular atrophy was considered—“SMA was characterized by symmetrical amyotrophy; however, the patient experienced long‐term amyotrophy and muscle weakness only in his right leg.” 2. Progressive muscular atrophy was considered—“As the disease progressed, symptoms of upper motor neuron damage developed, such as brisk reflexes and pathological signs,” ruling out a pure lower motor neuron syndrome.

title: Mycobacterium haemophilum Masquerading as Leprosy in a Renal Transplant Patient
case_prompt:
A 67‐year‐old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus and nephropathy presented with a 4‐month history of an asymptomatic rash and swelling over his right thigh. He had undergone a cadaveric renal transplant six years earlier and was maintained on cyclosporine, mycophenolic acid, and prednisone. He wore a prosthetic right lower limb after a below‐knee amputation; the eruption began distally on his residual limb and spread proximally. Empiric antifungal cream was ineffective, and topical corticosteroid worsened the lesions. He had traveled to Thailand, Vietnam, and Panama. 

On examination, there were multiple erythematous papules and plaques, some coalescing into an annular pattern on the right thigh. A punch biopsy showed dermal nonnecrotizing tuberculoid granulomas with lymphocytes and rare neutrophils. Acid‐fast staining revealed rare acid‐fast bacilli and demonstrated granulomatous involvement of a peripheral nerve bundle. Period acid‐Schiff stain was negative for fungal elements.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Deep fungal infection — “Period acid-Schiff stain was negative for fungal elements.” 2. Other nontuberculous mycobacteria — “AFB are usually present on acid-fast stains, but this pattern is not specific for any species of NTM.”

title: Idiopathic Infantile Arterial Calcification: A Possible Cause of Refractory Cardiopulmonary Failure in Infancy
case_prompt: A 4-week-old male infant, born at 30 weeks’ gestation (birth weight 1665 g; Apgar scores 8 and 9), was admitted at birth for respiratory distress and cyanosis requiring intubation. On initial examination, he had generalized edema and a grade III–IV holosystolic murmur. Chest radiographs and echocardiography revealed a moderate-to-large patent ductus arteriosus, moderate-to-severe pulmonary hypertension, and biventricular hypertrophy suggestive of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; a bedside PDA ligation was performed. Subsequent evaluation showed Coxsackie B viremia by PCR, and TORCH serologies were negative. He was treated with intravenous immunoglobulin, loop diuretics, a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor, and dopamine for presumed viral cardiomyopathy. Despite therapy, daily chest radiographs demonstrated progressive nonspecific calcification of the chest soft tissues and the vessels of both arms, and his condition worsened. He died at 4 weeks of age from intraventricular hemorrhage and multiorgan failure.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Congenital intrauterine infections were considered but were made unlikely because “none of which were present in our case. This in addition to the negative TORCH serology makes the classic group of congenitally acquired infections unlikely.” 2. Other causes of infant vascular calcification (eg, hypervitaminosis D, parathyroid abnormalities, progeria, Williams’s syndrome, pseudoxanthoma elasticum, and Singleton-Merten syndrome) were kept in mind but excluded because “these cases have different clinical presentations compared to IIAC.”

title: Diagnostic Dilemma of Vanishing Bone Disease - A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_prompt: A 35-year-old woman presented with a one-year history of mobile teeth in the lower left molar region and progressive difficulty chewing associated with pain and reduced mouth opening. She had hypothyroidism managed with medication; no history of trauma, familial bone disease, or systemic symptoms. On panoramic radiography, an initial patchy osteolysis of the left mandibular condyle was noted. Over 18 months, serial orthopantomograms demonstrated slowly progressive osteolysis extending from the condyle through the angle toward the mandibular body on the left side. Laboratory studies showed hypocalcemia (serum calcium 4.9 mg/dL), low vitamin D (52.14 nmol/L), and low intact parathyroid hormone (14.81 pg/mL). Investigations—including serologies and imaging—were performed to exclude rheumatoid arthritis, multiple myeloma, eosinophilic granuloma, and other systemic diseases. A whole-body PET scan revealed no additional lytic lesions. Cone-beam CT with three-dimensional reconstruction confirmed the extent of mandibular bone loss confined to the left hemi-mandible.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Rheumatoid arthritis was considered but excluded—“Investigations to rule out rheumatoid arthritis…were carried out.” 2. Multiple myeloma was considered but excluded—“Investigations to rule out…multiple myeloma…were carried out.” 3. Eosinophilic granuloma was considered but excluded—“Investigations to rule out…eosinophilic granuloma…were carried out.”

title: Congenital Pseudoarthrosis of Medial Malleolus in A Young Soccer Player - Diagnosis in Clinical setting of Ankle Sprain
case_prompt:
A 16-year-old female high–level soccer player twisted her right ankle during a match and immediately noted pain, swelling, and difficulty bearing weight. She had no prior history of tibial, tarsal, or ankle disease or injury. In the emergency department, radiographs of the right ankle were interpreted as a displaced medial malleolus fracture and demonstrated an os subfibularis. 

On transfer to our department, examination of the right ankle confirmed swelling, functional impairment, and intense medial malleolar pain but revealed no ankle laxity, instability, or pronation pain, and flexion-extension was preserved with only slight discomfort. Twenty-four hours later, she reported a marked reduction in pain and swelling and improved range of motion. 

To investigate the discrepancy between the initial radiographic interpretation and the unexpectedly benign clinical course, a comparison radiograph of the left (uninjured) ankle was obtained and showed a similarly shaped medial malleolus fragment without an os subfibularis. 

Because the clinical findings and bilateral radiographs were incongruent with a typical acute fracture, a bilateral CT scan was ordered.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute medial malleolus fracture was reconsidered because “there was no ankle relaxation, instability or pronation pain; furthermore the flexion-extension was preserved with slight pain. Twenty-four hours later a considerable remission of symptoms was evident: increase of the R.O.M. and reduction in the swelling and the post-traumatic edema.” 2. Heterotopic ossification was contemplated as an alternative cause of an accessory bone fragment since “Heterotypical ossification can occur in any malleolus. It is not known if they are present as an anatomical variation or as the consequence of chronic microtrauma.”
final_diagnosis: congenital pseudoarthrosis of the medial malleolus

title: Disseminated, fatal reactivation of bovine tuberculosis in a patient treated with adalimumab: a case report and review of the literature
case_prompt: A 78-year-old Swiss-born woman presented with a 10-kg weight loss, progressive weakness, and intermittent night sweats over 6 months. Her history was notable for seronegative rheumatoid polyarthritis treated with methotrexate and then leflunomide, switched to adalimumab in September 2021 after a negative interferon-gamma release assay and normal chest radiograph. On admission in April 2023, examination was notable for mild splenomegaly. Laboratory testing revealed hypercalcaemia and acute renal impairment. PET-CT showed multiple hypodense, intensely hypermetabolic lesions in the spleen and liver, small bilateral hypermetabolic pulmonary nodules, and hypermetabolic lymph nodes at the hepatic hilus. Splenic biopsy demonstrated non-necrotizing granulomatous splenitis. PCR for mycobacterial DNA and panfungal PCR from the biopsy were negative. In June 2023, systemic corticosteroids were started for suspected sarcoidosis, resulting in clinical improvement. Further evaluation showed elevated angiotensin-converting enzyme, soluble IL-2 receptor, and neopterin levels. Bronchoalveolar lavage cultures and lymph node biopsy showed no granulomas. Serologies for Treponema pallidum, HIV, Bartonella henselae, Coxiella burnetii, Francisella tularensis, Brucella spp., and dimorphic fungi were negative. A repeat T-SPOT.TB was positive. Liver biopsy again showed non-necrotizing granulomas; PCR and blood cultures for mycobacteria were negative. Adalimumab was stopped and steroids tapered, but hypercalcaemia and renal failure recurred, prompting re-initiation of steroids and adalimumab. In October 2023, she was readmitted with fever, dyspnoea, disorientation, and new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates on CT.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Sarcoidosis was strongly considered because of hypercalcaemia, elevated granulomatous markers, and steroid responsiveness — “Upon suspicion of sarcoidosis, systemic corticosteroid therapy was initiated with subsequent clinical improvement.” 2. Mycobacterial infection remained in the differential despite atypical histology because of TNF-blocker use and positive IGRA, but was initially ruled out by negative molecular and culture tests — “PCR targeting DNA for mycobacteria species, M. tuberculosis and panfungal PCR from the formalin-fixed biopsy material yielded negative results.” 3. Other granulomatous infections were excluded by serology — “Serological investigations for the following infectious agents returned negative results: Treponema pallidum, human immunodeficiency virus, Bartonella henselae, Coxiella burnetii, Francisella tularensis, Brucella spp., and dimorphic fungi.”

title: A case report of serpentine-like syndrome and review of literature
case_prompt:
A 34-year-old G2P0 woman, pregnant via artificial insemination, was referred for evaluation of a fetal thoracic cystic mass. First-trimester ultrasound showed a nuchal translucency of 1.8 mm, absence of the ductus venosus, and a cystic mass in the thoracic cavity. At 16–17 weeks, outside centers suggested either a simple cystic structure or congenital diaphragmatic hernia. At 19 weeks, 2D ultrasound at our center demonstrated:
• A cystic structure extending from the pharynx to the diaphragm in the right thoracic cavity  
• No gastric bubble in the abdominal cavity  
• The hepatic portal vein traversing behind the intrathoracic structure into the right atrium  
• A possible esophagobronchial fistula  

At 21 weeks, fetal MRI showed:
• A large cystic mass in the right thoracic cavity with no stomach in the abdomen or intestinal filling  
• Absence of the diaphragm in the lower posterior region of the heart, consistent with diaphragmatic hernia  
• Right lung hypoplasia with a small pleural effusion  
• Possible esophageal atresia with tracheoesophageal fistula  
• Suggested interruption of continuity in the cervical vertebral bodies  

At 23 weeks, three-dimensional ultrasound with Crystal Vue revealed:
• A defect in the middle and posterior diaphragm allowing partial herniation of abdominal organs into the chest  
• A markedly shortened esophagus (1.5 cm in length)  
• The distal esophagus directly connected to a cystic mass, confirming an intrathoracic stomach
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Congenital diaphragmatic hernia — “In all CDH types, the abdominal organs that herniated into the thoracic cavity are not fixed.” 2. Pulmonary sequestration — “The blood vessels of pulmonary sequestration [arise] from the aorta and the blood supply of other thoracic diseases [is] from the thoracic vessels.” 3. Esophageal hiatal hernia — “In SLS and oesophageal hiatal hernia, the diaphragmatic defects are in the middle and posterior side of the diaphragm.” 4. Congenital lung lesions — “The size and location of the stomach are normal in congenital lung lesions.”

title: Acute aseptic meningitis as the initial presentation of a macroprolactinoma
case_prompt:
A 31-year-old man presented with a 6-day history of severe headache and vomiting. He reported a progressive loss of vision in his left eye and decreased libido over the preceding two years. He denied any rhinorrhea, trauma, or prior neurosurgery. On examination, he was alert, febrile (38.0 °C), and had complete blindness of the left eye without other cranial-nerve deficits; no nasal discharge was noted.

Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein level of 305 mg/L (normal <10 mg/L), a leukocyte count of 13,000/μL, serum sodium 131 mEq/L, and blood glucose 127 mg/dL. Hormonal assays revealed a prolactin level of 9000 ng/mL (normal 2.5–11 ng/mL) with additional findings consistent with panhypopituitarism.

Computed tomography of the head demonstrated a large sellar mass with erosion of the sellar floor and extension into the sphenoid sinus. Magnetic resonance imaging confirmed a suprasellar and infrasellar lesion extending into the cavernous sinuses, encroaching on the third ventricle, and compressing the optic chiasm, without evidence of intratumoral hemorrhage.

A lumbar puncture yielded purulent cerebrospinal fluid containing 11,900 white blood cells/μL, 2,680 red blood cells/μL, protein 6.3 g/L, glucose 39 mg/dL, and lactate 81 mg/dL.  
What is your differential diagnosis?
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Bacterial meningitis from a CSF leak — “Meningitis in a patient with an invasive pituitary macroadenoma is generally due to an infection of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leaking through the disrupted bony skull into the sphenoid sinus, allowing the entry of nasopharyngeal organisms.” 2. Pituitary apoplexy — excluded because “No intratumoral hemorrhage was observed.”

title: Swift Onset, Swift Recovery: Unusual Nonrheumatic Myocarditis in a Young Adult Post Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis
case_prompt: A 24-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with nonradiating central chest pain of a few hours’ duration. One day earlier, he had been diagnosed with group A streptococcal pharyngitis and started on amoxicillin 500 mg orally twice daily. On initial evaluation, his heart rate was 116 beats per minute; other vital signs were normal. In urgent care, an electrocardiogram showed sinus bradycardia with T-wave inversions in leads III, aVR, V1, and V2 and ST-segment depressions in leads aVR and V1. He was transferred to the emergency department, where bradycardia persisted but other vital signs remained unremarkable. A single dose of aspirin 324 mg was given. Laboratory testing revealed an elevated troponin T concentration. A heparin infusion was initiated, and cardiology was consulted. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated only trace tricuspid and mitral regurgitation. Repeat ECGs were unchanged, and no evidence of acute coronary syndrome was seen.
diagnostic_reasoning: 1. Acute coronary syndrome — “Based on their evaluation, it was felt that the patient most likely had myocarditis and less likely acute coronary syndrome.” 2. Acute rheumatic fever — “Based on their evaluation and timing of chest pain just one day after the diagnosis of streptococcal pharyngitis, it was felt that the patient was likely experiencing nonrheumatic myocarditis rather than acute rheumatic fever…” 3. Viral myocarditis — “…although concurrent viral myocarditis was thought to be a possibility.”
